text: If you're Canadian, keep watching. It's election time. I've just requested that the Governor General dissolve Parliament and call an election. She has agreed. The campaign will last thirty-six days, and the election will be on April twenty-eighth. There are three hundred forty-three ridings up for grabs across the country. That's five more than the last election. Each one has a corresponding seat in the House of Commons. Here's what the House looks like right now, with the Liberal minority government. The Liberals have a hundred fifty-two seats. The Conservatives have a hundred and twenty. The Bloc Québécois have thirty-three. The NDP have twenty-four. The Green Party has two. Three MPs are sitting as independents, and four seats are vacant. For a majority government, a party needs to win at least a hundred and seventy-two seats. But a minority government can be formed with fewer than that. Mark Carney has only been Prime Minister for about a week after he won the Liberal leadership, and Justin Trudeau officially resigned. If the Liberal Party loses this election, Carney would be Canada's shortest-serving Prime Minister. That title is currently held by this guy, Charles Tupper, who was Prime Minister for sixty-nine days in eighteen ninety-six. Now in Canada, we don't vote directly for the Prime Minister. We vote for a local member of Parliament, and the leader of the party with the most MPs elected becomes Prime Minister. The main party leaders are Mark Carney for the Liberals, Pierre Poilievre for the Conservatives, Jagmeet Singh for the NDP, Eve Fung-Swablanche for the Bloc Québécois, and Elizabeth May and Jonathan Pachno co-lead the Green Party. Currently, Carney doesn't have a seat in the House of Commons, and he's never held elected office before. He's running in the Ottawa riding of the Peel. You can look up your riding on the Elections Canada website. To vote, you need to be a Canadian citizen, at least eighteen years old on election day, and have proof of your identity and address. You can vote on election day or earlier during the advance polls. You can also request a mail-in ballot if you're outside of the country. You'll be able to find all that information on the Elections Canada website once it's available. And leading up to election day, we'll be answering your questions. So tell us what you'd like to know about the party platforms and the issues that matter
